preFix.title: Whittled ash butter knife
preFix.subtitle: A simple flat-bladed spreading knife in green ash
preFix.excerpt: A whittled ash butter knife with a flat flexible blade and a rounded handle. About 18 cm finished length. Requires only a sloyd knife and a piece of straight-grained ash. A first-session project for anyone new to whittling.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for single-knife carving method. The butter knife is documented in Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) and in early-twentieth-century woodcraft manuals.

content.text: A butter knife is the best first whittling project. The shape is simple, the blade section needs no hollowing, and the whole thing can be made with a
sloyd knife
alone. Ash is a good starting wood: the grain is visible and easy to read, it carves cleanly in
green
condition, and it dries food-safe. A straight-grained piece 20 cm long and 25 mm square is the starting material.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

Ash is the classic whittling wood in the British tradition. The grain lines are bold and parallel, making it easy to see which way the cuts should run. Ash carves cleanly green with a sharp edge and dries hard and pale.
If ash is unavailable, sycamore is the easier substitute (softer, smoother grain). Hazel is another option from hedgerow coppice.
Shaping the blank
The
blank

is a 20 cm by 25 mm square section. Draw the outline on one flat face: a blade 10 cm long tapering from 18 mm at the shoulder to 12 mm at the tip, and a handle 10 cm long tapering from 20 mm at the shoulder to 14 mm at the pommel.

Use push cuts to reduce the handle to an oval cross-section first. Work from the shoulder toward the pommel: with the grain, clean cuts. Then use push cuts on the blade section to taper it from 18 mm at the shoulder down to 5 mm at the tip. The blade of a butter knife is not a cutting edge; it is a flexible spreading surface, so taper only the thickness, not the width.
Refining

Use thumb-pivot cuts to refine the shoulder where blade meets handle. This is the tightest curve on the piece. Work from the shoulder outward in both directions; never cut across the shoulder from one section into the other in one stroke.
Round all edges slightly. A butter knife with sharp square edges is uncomfortable to hold and liable to split along the corners as it dries.
Drying and finishing

Dry in a paper bag for two to three weeks. Board butter (beeswax and food-grade mineral oil) is the right finish for a butter knife: it conditions the wood, re-coats easily, and is food-safe from the first application. Rub it in with a cloth, let it stand twenty minutes, then buff off the surplus.
Variations
Jam spreader: widen the blade to 20 mm and keep it thicker (8 mm) for more stiffness.
Kolrosing on the handle: once dry, inscribe a simple geometric pattern using the kolrosing technique and rub in ground coffee for a dark contrast. See the kolrosing technique tutorial.
Care
Hand wash and towel-dry. Re-apply board butter once a month or when the wood looks dry. No dishwasher.

rows.fix: 3 mm minimum at the tip. For cracks that appear, fill with wood glue, clamp lightly, and let dry. A cracked blade is decorative only.
rows.cause: Blade too thin at the tip (under 3 mm) or dried too fast
rows.symptom: Blade section cracked along the grain when drying

rows.fix: On ash, the grain lines are bold enough to read clearly. Cuts that travel with the grain produce shavings; cuts against the grain lift fibres. Reverse direction.
rows.symptom: Push cuts tear the surface on the blade section

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
